questions.title: According to Plato Wisdom is the special virtue of the?
questions.explanation: According to Plato, Wisdom is the special virtue of the Ruling class.

questions.title: Which comprehends all cardinal virtues?
questions.explanation: Justice comprehends all cardinal virtues.

questions.title: Which of the following is not a Cardinal virtue?
questions.explanation: "Goodness" is not considered a Cardinal virtue.

questions.title: 'Cardinal virtues'-related with?
questions.explanation: 'Cardinal virtues' are related to Plato.

questions.title: "Virtue is knowledge"-related with?
questions.explanation: Virtue as knowledge is a concept related to Socrates.

questions.title: Virtue is a permanent state of mind. Who holds this position?
questions.explanation: Aristotle holds the position that virtue is a permanent state of mind.

questions.title: Which is the habit of deliberate choice of wrong actions?
questions.explanation: The habit of deliberate choice of wrong actions is called Vice.

questions.title: Which is expressed in the commission of sins?
questions.explanation: Vice is expressed in the commission of sins.

questions.title: Virtue is the habit of deliberate choice of?
questions.explanation: Virtue is the habit of deliberate choice of Right actions.

questions.title: Which is the excellence of character?
questions.explanation: The excellence of character is Virtue.

questions.title: Virtue denotes a?
questions.explanation: Virtue denotes a Good Character.

questions.title: Duties are turned into virtues by?
questions.explanation: Duties are turned into virtues by Habit.

questions.title: Duties of imperfect obligation are called?
questions.explanation: Duties of imperfect obligation are called Virtues.

questions.title: ‘Vir’ means?
questions.explanation: 'Vir' means Man/Hero.

questions.title: We should cultivate fellow feelings-related with?
questions.explanation: "We should cultivate fellow feelings" is related to Respect for World Harmony.

questions.title: We should maintain the social system to which we belong. We should not revolt against the State and produce chaos-related with?
questions.explanation: "We should maintain the social system to which we belong. We should not revolt against the State and produce chaos" is related to Respect for Society.

questions.title: We should keep our promises and fulfil our contracts-related with?
questions.explanation: "We should keep our promises and fulfill our contracts" is related to Respect for Truth.

questions.title: "Be a person and respect others as persons". Assertion is related with?
questions.explanation: "Be a person and respect others as persons". Assertion is related with Hengel.

questions.title: “Thou shall not kill”. The maxim related with?
questions.explanation: "Thou shall not kill" is a maxim related to Respect for Life.

questions.title: The right of contract necessarily arises out of the?
questions.explanation: The right of contract necessarily arises out of the Right of Property.

questions.title: Right to employment should be recognised by every?
questions.explanation: Right to employment should be recognized by every Welfare state.

questions.title: Rights of property are essentially personal. Who hold this position?
questions.explanation: Hegel held the position that "Rights of property are essentially personal."

questions.title: The sacredness of life should be recognised in?
questions.explanation: The sacredness of life should be recognized in the Right to live.

questions.title: The first right of man is?
questions.explanation: The first right of man is generally considered to be the right to live.

questions.title: Rights and duties are?
questions.explanation: Rights and duties are Correlative to each other.

questions.title: Which are indispensible for the realization of the highest good and common good?
questions.explanation: Moral rights are indispensable for the realization of the highest good and common good.

questions.title: Duties are?
questions.explanation: Duties are Obligations.

questions.title: Right is a?
questions.explanation: Right is a Justifiable claim.

questions.title: Duties are_________of individuals recognised by society.
questions.explanation: Duties are Obligations of individuals recognized by society.

questions.title: Rights are________of individuals recognised by society.
questions.explanation: Rights are Moral claims of individuals recognized by society.

questions.title: Sidgwick considers knowledge, beauty, and virtue are?
questions.explanation: Sidgwick considers knowledge, beauty, and virtue as Means to pleasure.

questions.title: Sidgwick holds that_______is the only intrinsic value.
questions.explanation: Sidgwick holds that Pleasure is the only intrinsic value.

questions.title: Hennery Sidgwick’s ethical position is known as?
questions.explanation: Henry Sidgwick's ethical position is known as "Rational Utilitarianism."

questions.title: Rogers calls Butler’s doctrine as?
questions.explanation: Rogers calls Butler's doctrine "Autonomic intuitionism."

questions.title: “Man is a law to himself”. Who asserts this?
questions.explanation: "Man is a law to himself" is indeed asserted by Butler.

questions.title: According to Butler Conscience is?
questions.explanation: According to Butler, Conscience is Categorical.

questions.title: Bishop Butler regarded_______as the highest principle in human nature.
questions.explanation: Bishop Butler regarded Conscience as the highest principle in human nature.

questions.title: Butler’s ethical position is called as?
questions.explanation:
Butler's Intuitionism posits that moral principles are known intuitively through introspection rather than through reason or empirical observation.

It emphasizes the importance of moral intuition as the foundation for ethical judgments.

According to Butler, humans possess a natural faculty that allows them to discern right from wrong intuitively, without relying on external sources or complex reasoning processes.

questions.title: Moral progress is the gradual reproduction of divine perfection in man. Who holds this view?
questions.explanation: T.H. Green holds the view that "moral progress is the gradual reproduction of divine perfection in man."

questions.title: T. H. Green holds that there is a_______in nature.
questions.explanation: T. H. Green holds that there is a spiritual principle in nature.

questions.title: To realise the completely rational universe is to realize the true self. Who holds this view?
questions.explanation: T.H. Green holds this view. He emphasizes the importance of realizing the completely rational universe as a means to realizing the true self.

questions.title: Spencer argues that absolute ethics deals with the absolute morality in______society.
questions.explanation: Spencer argues that absolute ethics deals with the absolute morality in a perfect society.

questions.title: Spencer argues that Relative ethics deals with relative morality in______society.
questions.explanation: Spencer argues that Relative ethics deals with relative morality in an imperfect society.

questions.title: Spencer distinguishes between Absolute ethics and?
questions.explanation: Spencer distinguishes between Absolute ethics and Relative ethics.

questions.title: According to Herbert Spencer Self-preservation and race- preservation are the ultimate ends of?
questions.explanation: According to Herbert Spencer, self-preservation and race-preservation are the ultimate ends of biological evolution.

questions.title: Thus pleasure is both______and ethically good.
questions.explanation: Thus pleasure is both biologically and ethically good.

questions.title: According to F. H. Bradley, performance of duties appropriate to ones station will leads to?
questions.explanation: According to F.H. Bradley, the performance of duties appropriate to one's station will lead to self-realization.

questions.title: 'My station and its Duties' is related with?
questions.explanation:
"My station and its Duties" is related to F.H. Bradley.

He explored this concept in his ethical philosophy, emphasizing the idea that moral obligations are determined by an individual's social role or position.

questions.title: Perfectionism is also called?
questions.explanation: Perfectionism is also called Eudemonism.

questions.title: Pleasure-giving acts are life-sustaining; pain-giving acts are life-destroying. Who holds this position?
questions.explanation: Herbert Spencer holds this position. He argues that pleasure-giving acts sustain life, while pain-giving acts destroy life.

questions.title: Pleasure in an index of increase of life; pain is an index of decrease of life. Who advocates this view?
questions.explanation: Herbert Spencer advocates this view. He argues that pleasure indicates an increase in life or vitality, while pain indicates a decrease in life or vitality.

questions.title: Spencer defines______as the adjustment of acts to ends.
questions.explanation:
Spencer's definition of conduct implies that it involves the deliberate alignment of actions with specific goals or purposes.

It suggests a conscious effort to achieve desired outcomes through one's behavior.

This perspective emphasizes the intentional aspect of human actions in achieving their objectives.

questions.title: Morality is a product of evolution. Who holds this view?
questions.explanation: Spencer is known for holding the view that morality is a product of evolution.

questions.title: Spencer deduces hedonism from the law of?
questions.explanation: Spencer deduces hedonism from the law of biological evolution.

questions.title: Who deduces hedonism from the law of biological evolution?
questions.explanation:
Herbert Spencer is known for deducing hedonism from the law of biological evolution.

He argued that the evolution of organisms is driven by a pursuit of pleasure and avoidance of pain, aligning with the principles of hedonism.

questions.title: Herbert Spencer deduces moral laws from?
questions.explanation:
Herbert Spencer deduced moral laws from biological laws.

He believed that ethical principles could be derived from observations of biological processes and principles, which he termed "biological laws."

questions.title: ‘Data of Ethics’ is related with?
questions.explanation:
The concept of the "Data of Ethics" is associated with Herbert Spencer.

Herbert Spencer, a British philosopher and sociologist, discussed the "data of ethics" in his work "The Principles of Ethics."

He explored how ethical principles could be derived from observable social and biological phenomena.

questions.title: The hedonism of Herbert Spencer is called?
questions.explanation:
Herbert Spencer's "Evolutionary hedonism" posits that pleasure and pain drive human behavior and are subject to evolutionary pressures.

According to Spencer, individuals seek pleasure and avoid pain in ways that promote their survival and reproductive success, aligning with the principles of natural selection.

This perspective suggests that hedonistic tendencies have evolved over time to enhance fitness and adaptation, shaping human behavior in response to environmental challenges and opportunities.

questions.title: Bradley argues that morality is?
questions.explanation:
F. H. Bradley asserts that morality is objective, meaning it is grounded in universal principles and truths that apply universally to all individuals regardless of personal beliefs or preferences.

According to Bradley, moral standards exist independently of subjective opinions or individual perspectives, guiding ethical conduct based on inherent principles of right and wrong.

This objective view of morality emphasizes the importance of adhering to timeless moral principles that transcend individual preferences and cultural differences, promoting consistency and coherence in ethical decision-making.

questions.title: Bradley means by self-realization, realization of the?
questions.explanation:
Bradley's concept of self-realization involves transcending the limitations of the finite self and connecting with a deeper, more expansive aspect of one's being, which he refers to as the "Infinite self."

This notion suggests that true self-realization entails recognizing and embracing the boundless potential and interconnectedness of the human spirit beyond individual limitations.

By attaining awareness of the Infinite self, individuals can achieve a profound sense of fulfillment and alignment with universal truths, contributing to their holistic development and spiritual growth.

questions.title: According to F. H. Bradley personal morality apart from social morality is an?
questions.explanation:
F. H. Bradley contends that personal morality divorced from social morality exists merely as an abstraction, lacking practical significance or efficacy.

In his view, morality is inherently social, shaped by collective norms, values, and interactions within society.

questions.title: F. H. Bradley claims that_____is never possible apart from society.
questions.explanation:
F.H. Bradley argues that individuals achieve self-realization through their interactions and connections within society, as it provides the necessary context for personal growth and development.

According to Bradley, the fulfillment of one's potential and the realization of one's true self are inherently linked to participation in social structures and relationships.

Thus, he asserts that self-realization is a product of communal engagement and cannot be fully attained in isolation from society.

questions.title: The highest personal good is in harmony with the highest social good. Who claims this notion?
questions.explanation:
F.H. Bradley asserts that individual fulfillment and societal well-being are inherently intertwined, suggesting that pursuing one's highest personal good naturally aligns with promoting the highest social good.

This perspective emphasizes the interconnectedness of personal flourishing and the welfare of the broader community, highlighting the importance of harmonizing individual pursuits with collective interests for the greater benefit of society.

Bradley's notion underscores the idea that achieving personal fulfillment is not at odds with contributing positively to the welfare of others and society as a whole.

questions.title: According to F. H. Bradley different_____are organs of the moral organism.
questions.explanation: According to F. H. Bradley, different "Individuals" are organs of the moral organism.

questions.title: F. H. Bradley asserts that, society is a?
questions.explanation:
F. H. Bradley conceptualizes society as a "moral organism," suggesting that it functions akin to a living entity with its own moral character and principles.

In this view, society operates according to collective moral standards and values that guide its actions and interactions.

Bradley emphasizes the interconnectedness of individuals within society, highlighting how their moral conduct contributes to the overall moral fabric of the collective entity.

This perspective underscores the importance of moral considerations in understanding and evaluating societal dynamics and behavior.

questions.title: His duties are determined by his station in society. Who said this?
questions.explanation:
F.H. Bradley argues that an individual's duties are intrinsically tied to their social position or station in society.

According to Bradley, one's role within the social hierarchy dictates the specific obligations and responsibilities they have towards others and the community.

This perspective implies that each person has distinct duties based on their station, and fulfilling these duties contributes to the overall harmony and functioning of society.

questions.title: F. H. Bradley argues that each member has a particular station in society determined by his peculiar?
questions.explanation:
F. H. Bradley posits that individuals possess unique talents or abilities that determine their roles and responsibilities within society.

According to his view, each person's talents shape their specific station or position in society, guiding them towards fulfilling roles where their skills are most valuable.

This perspective suggests that individuals should recognize and embrace their talents, contributing to society according to their inherent abilities.

questions.title: Ones duties relate to his own station in life. Who argues this?
questions.explanation: The argument that "one's duties relate to his own station in life" is often associated with F.H. Bradley.

questions.title: The Greek term "eudaimonia" means?
questions.explanation: The Greek term "eudaimonia" means "Happiness."

questions.title: Self-realisation is the highest good, this notion is known called?
questions.explanation:
Perfectionism is an ethical theory that emphasizes the pursuit of self-realization and the development of one's full potential as the highest good.

According to this view, individuals should strive for excellence in all aspects of life, including moral, intellectual, and personal growth.

Perfectionists believe that achieving self-fulfillment leads to a flourishing and meaningful existence, and they prioritize self-improvement as a fundamental aspect of ethical living.

questions.title: According to Kant duty as the fundamental concept of ethics, this view is called?
questions.explanation:
Kantian deontology is an ethical theory that emphasizes the importance of duty and moral obligation as the foundation of ethical decision-making.

According to Kant, actions are morally right if they are done out of a sense of duty, regardless of the consequences or outcomes.

In Kant's view, duty is determined by rational principles and the categorical imperative, which instructs individuals to act in accordance with universal moral laws.

Thus, Kantian deontology prioritizes adherence to moral duty over considerations of personal interest or the potential consequences of actions.

questions.title: Kant assumes that_____are the only two motives of actions.
questions.explanation: Kant assumes that "Duty and self-interest" are the only two motives of actions.

questions.title: Kant’s ethical theory is called?
questions.explanation: Kant's ethical theory is called "Deontology."

questions.title: Kant’s ethical doctrine is called?
questions.explanation: Kant's ethical doctrine is called "Formalism."

questions.title: ‘Freewill is implied by morality’. Who argues this?
questions.explanation: Kant argues that "Freewill is implied by morality."

questions.title: According to Kant______is the fundamental postulate of morality.
questions.explanation: According to Kant, "Freedom of the will" is the fundamental postulate of morality.

questions.title: Kant argues that Categorical Imperative is the______it applies to all persons.
questions.explanation: Kant argues that the Categorical Imperative is the "Universal moral law" as it applies to all persons universally.

questions.title: According to Kant a natural law is?
questions.explanation: According to Kant, a natural law is "Assertorial."

questions.title: According to Kant the moral law is a?
questions.explanation: According to Kant, the moral law is a "Categorical imperative."

questions.title: According to Immanuel Kant, moral law is?
questions.explanation: According to Immanuel Kant, moral law is "Self evident."

questions.title: According to Immanuel Kant moral law is?
questions.explanation: According to Immanuel Kant, moral law is "A priori."

questions.title: According to Immanuel Kant the moral law is known?
questions.explanation: According to Immanuel Kant, the moral law is known "Intuitively."

questions.title: The laws of ethics can be violated but cannot be?
questions.explanation: The laws of ethics can be violated but cannot be "Changed."

questions.title: Laws of______can be violated but cannot be changed.
questions.explanation: Laws of ethics can be violated but generally cannot be changed, as they are based on moral principles and norms.

questions.title: Mill’s theory of Hedonism focuses on the quality of the pleasure, rather than the quantity, so it is called?
questions.explanation: Mill's theory of Hedonism focuses on the quality of the pleasure rather than the quantity, so it is called "Qualitative hedonism."

questions.title: Mill justified______pleasures are more valuable than bodily pleasures.
questions.explanation: Mill justified "Intellectual" pleasures as more valuable than bodily pleasures.

questions.title: According to J.S. Mill______pleasures are those associated with the body.
questions.explanation: According to J.S. Mill, "Lower" pleasures are those associated with the body.

questions.title: According to J.S. Mill______pleasures are those associated with the mind.
questions.explanation: According to J.S. Mill, "Higher" pleasures are those associated with the mind.

questions.title: According to J.S. Mill higher pleasures are those associated with the?
questions.explanation: According to J.S. Mill, higher pleasures are those associated with the "Mind".

questions.title: According to J.S. Mill Lower pleasures are those associated with the?
questions.explanation: According to J.S. Mill, lower pleasures are those associated with the "Body."

questions.title: Mill argued that pleasures could vary in?
questions.explanation: Mill argued that pleasures could vary in "Quality."

questions.title: Mill argues that_______are superior to physical forms of pleasure.
questions.explanation: Mill argues that "Moral pleasures" are superior to physical forms of pleasure.

questions.title: J. S. Mill’s version of hedonism is known as?
questions.explanation: J.S. Mill's version of hedonism is known as "Qualitative hedonism."

questions.title: Bentham’s version of hedonism is known as?
questions.explanation: Bentham's version of hedonism is known as "Quantitative hedonism."

questions.title: The hedonism which, focuses the quantity of the pleasure, rather than the quality is called?
questions.explanation: The hedonism that focuses on the quantity of pleasure rather than the quality is called "Quantitative hedonism."

questions.title: Which refers to the felt strength of the pleasure or pain?
questions.explanation: The term that refers to the felt strength of the pleasure or pain is "Intensity."

questions.title: Hedonic Calculus of Bentham is a______calculus.
questions.explanation:
The seven points in Bentham's Hedonic Calculus are:

Intensity: How strong is the pleasure or pain?

Duration: How long will the pleasure or pain last?

Certainty: How likely is it that the pleasure or pain will occur?

Propinquity: How soon will the pleasure or pain occur?

Fecundity: The likelihood that the pleasure or pain will lead to more of the same sensation.

Purity: The likelihood that the pleasure or pain will lead to some of the opposite sensation.

Extent: How many people will be affected by the pleasure or pain?

questions.title: Which refers to how long the pleasure or pain are felt for?
questions.explanation: The term that refers to how long the pleasure or pain are felt for is "Duration."

questions.title: Which refers to the number of people the pleasure or pain is likely to affect?
questions.explanation: The term that refers to the number of people the pleasure or pain is likely to affect is "Extent."

questions.title: Which refers to the likelihood of the pleasure or pain leading to some of the opposite sensation?
questions.explanation: The term that refers to the likelihood of the pleasure or pain leading to some of the opposite sensation is "Purity."

questions.title: Which refers to the likelihood of the pleasure or pain leading to more of the same sensation?
questions.explanation: The term that refers to the likelihood of the pleasure or pain leading to more of the same sensation is "Fecundity."

questions.title: Which refers to how long away the pleasure or pain is?
questions.explanation: The term that refers to how long away the pleasure or pain is "Propinquity."

questions.title: Which refers to the likelihood that the pleasure or pain will occur?
questions.explanation: Certainty refers to the likelihood that the pleasure or pain will occur.

questions.title: Who introduced the method of hedonic calculus?
questions.explanation: The method of hedonic calculus was introduced by Jeremy Bentham.

questions.title: The Hedonic Calculus is a method of counting the amount of______that would likely be caused by different actions.
questions.explanation: The Hedonic Calculus is indeed a method of counting the amount of pleasure and pain that would likely be caused by different actions.

questions.title: Which is a method of counting the amount of pleasure and pain that would likely be caused by different actions?
questions.explanation: "Hedonistic calculus": It's a method proposed by Jeremy Bentham for calculating the amount of pleasure and pain generated by different actions, ultimately to determine the most ethical course of action

questions.title: Utilitarian school having the slogan?
questions.answer: The greatest happiness of greatest number
questions.explanation: The slogan commonly associated with the Utilitarian school of thought is "The greatest happiness of the greatest number."

questions.title: Universalistic Ethical Hedonism is also known as?
questions.explanation: Universalistic Ethical Hedonism is also known as Utilitarianism.

questions.title: The most influential contributor to Universal ethical hedonism is?
questions.explanation: The most influential contributor to Universal ethical hedonism is John Stuart Mill.

questions.title: The theory which holds that the right action is the one that produces the greatest happiness for all is?
questions.explanation:
Utilitarianism is a theory of morality that advocates actions that foster happiness and oppose actions that cause unhappiness.

Utilitarianism promotes "the greatest amount of good for the greatest number of people."

questions.title: Each man seeks, or ought to seek, is his own pleasure, this type of ethical hedonism is called?
questions.explanation: Each man seeks, or ought to seek, his own pleasure, is known as Egoistic ethical hedonism.

questions.title: Man ought to seeks pleasure, the position is known as?
questions.explanation: The position that man ought to seek pleasure is known as Ethical hedonism.

questions.title: Men always seeks pleasure-this position is known as?
questions.explanation:
Psychological hedonism asserts that humans are inherently motivated by the pursuit of pleasure and the avoidance of pain.

According to this position, all human actions, whether conscious or unconscious, are ultimately driven by the desire for pleasure.

It suggests that seeking pleasure is a fundamental aspect of human nature, influencing decision-making and behavior across various contexts.

questions.title: According to the nature of seeking pleasure hedonism can be divided broadly in to Psychological hedonism and?
questions.explanation: According to the nature of seeking pleasure, hedonism can be broadly divided into Psychological hedonism and Ethical hedonism.

questions.title: Hedonism is a school of thought that argues that______is the only intrinsic good.
questions.explanation: Hedonism is a school of thought that argues that pleasure is the only intrinsic good.

questions.title: Hedone means?
questions.explanation: Hedone means pleasure.

questions.title: Hedonism the term derives from the Greek word?
questions.explanation: The term "Hedonism" derives from the Greek word "Hedone," meaning pleasure.

questions.title: Which is the general term for the theories that regards happiness?
questions.explanation:
Hedonism Theory: In all its variants, it holds that happiness is a matter of raw subjective feeling.

A happy life maximizes feelings of pleasure and minimizes pain.

questions.title: Human beings share some kinds of needs with the lower animals and even with plants are called?
questions.explanation:
Organic needs refer to fundamental requirements shared among humans, lower animals, and plants for basic survival and functioning.

These needs include essentials like nourishment, hydration, and shelter, which are essential for sustaining life across various species.

Understanding organic needs underscores the interconnectedness and commonality in the biological requirements for existence.

questions.title: McDougall defines an instinct as______psycho physical disposition.
questions.explanation:
McDougall's definition suggests that instincts are inherited tendencies present at birth.

These tendencies influence both psychological and physical behavior, guiding individuals toward certain actions or responses.

Instincts are deeply ingrained and evolved traits that contribute to survival and adaptation in various environments.

questions.title: Sucking of the thump by a child is a?
questions.explanation:
Sucking the thumb by a child often becomes a habitual action as it provides comfort and security.

Over time, the repetitive nature of this behavior reinforces its occurrence.

Habitual thumb sucking can be a self-soothing mechanism, especially during times of stress or boredom.

questions.title: A motive is________mental process.
questions.explanation: A motive is a conscious mental process, involving the awareness and deliberation of one's reasons or intentions behind an action.

questions.title: When a man acts blindly without considering the end or result of his action. Such actions is called?
questions.explanation:
Impulsive action refers to behavior that occurs spontaneously without thoughtful consideration of consequences or outcomes.

It often arises from sudden urges or emotions, leading individuals to act impulsively without weighing the potential results.

Such actions are characterized by their lack of deliberate planning or foresight.

questions.title: An intention which the agent does not definitely avows to himself is called?
questions.explanation:
An unconscious intention refers to a motive or purpose behind an action that the agent is not consciously aware of or does not explicitly acknowledge.

It may drive behavior without the individual's conscious recognition, yet still influences their actions.

Understanding unconscious intentions can provide insight into subconscious motivations and behaviors.

questions.title: Hobbes asserted that human desire is the______of all human action.
questions.explanation: Hobbes asserted that human desire is the Fundamental motivation of all human action.

questions.title: Desire is a sense of longing of a person for?
questions.explanation: Desire is a sense of longing of a person for an outcome.

questions.title: A person’s conduct corresponding to his?
questions.explanation: A person's conduct corresponds to his Character, which encompasses their values, beliefs, and habitual patterns of behavior.

questions.title: The motive means, of course, what moves us or causes us to act in a particular way. Who asserted this?
questions.explanation: "The motive means, of course, what moves us or causes us to act in a particular way" is attributed to Mackenzie.

questions.title: The remote intention of an act is sometimes called?
questions.explanation:
The remote intention of an act, also known as the motive, refers to the underlying reason or driving force behind an action.

It represents the broader goal or objective that motivates a person's behavior, often influencing their decisions and actions over an extended period.

Understanding one's motives can provide insight into their behavior and decision-making processes.

questions.title: The particular result as a realised fact is?
questions.explanation: The particular result as a realized fact is Material intention.

questions.title: According to Mackenzie the formal intention means the______embodied in fact.
questions.explanation: According to Mackenzie, the formal intention means the Purpose embodied in fact.

questions.title: The whole willed scheme of action, which is anticipated by the doer is called?
questions.explanation: The whole willed scheme of action, which is anticipated by the doer, is called Intention.

questions.title: The term "intention" corresponds the term?
questions.explanation: The term "intention" corresponds to the term Purpose.

questions.title: Which is a conscious mental process which moves a man to act in a particular way?
questions.explanation: The conscious mental process which moves a person to act in a particular way is called a Motive.

questions.title: The desires that predominate or continue to be effective is called?
questions.explanation: The desires that predominate or continue to be effective are called Will.

questions.title: Who defines an instinct as an inherited psycho physical disposition?
questions.explanation:
William McDougall defines an instinct as an inherited psychophysical disposition.

William McDougall "The Nature of Instincts and Their Place in the Constitution of the Human Mind."

questions.title: Consciousness of______is called an appetite.
questions.explanation: Consciousness of an organic need is called an appetite.

questions.title: ‘Human desire is the fundamental motivation of all human action’. Whose view is this?
questions.explanation: "Human desire is the fundamental motivation of all human action" is associated with Thomas Hobbes, a philosopher known for his social contract theory and his work on political philosophy.

questions.title: Which is a sense of longing for a person or object or hoping for an outcome?
questions.explanation:
Desires are states of mind that are expressed by terms like "wanting", "wishing", "longing" or "craving".

A great variety of features is commonly associated with desires.

questions.title: Which is a collective name for voluntary actions?
questions.explanation: Conduct is a collective name for voluntary actions.

questions.title: Morality consists of goodness, which is really an______end.
questions.explanation: Morality consists of goodness, which is really an Intrinsic end.

questions.title: Ethics considers the______of goodness.
questions.explanation: Ethics considers the Standard of goodness.

questions.title: Ethics investigates the nature of its fundamental notions like right, duty and?
questions.explanation: Ethics investigates the nature of its fundamental notions like right, duty, and good.

questions.title: "Logic, Ethic and Aesthetics" these three sciences are essentially?
questions.explanation: "Logic, Ethics, and Aesthetics" are essentially Cognate sciences, meaning they are closely related or connected, often studied together due to their interconnectedness in understanding human reasoning, conduct, and appreciation of beauty.

questions.title: Ethics is concerned the______to judge human conduct.
questions.explanation: Ethics is concerned with the Standards to judge human conduct.

questions.title: Ethics deals with the standards to describe?
questions.explanation: Ethics deals with the standards to describe Right and good, guiding individuals and societies on what actions are morally right or wrong.

questions.title: ______is not a normative discipline.
questions.explanation: Metaphysics is not a normative discipline.

questions.title: Ethics deals with the correctness of?
questions.explanation: Ethics deals with the correctness of Conduct, determining what actions are morally right or wrong.

questions.title: Logic deals with the correctness of?
questions.explanation: Logic deals with the correctness of Thought, focusing on the structure and validity of reasoning processes.

questions.title: Positive science deals with judgments of facts while ethics is concerned with judgments of?
questions.explanation: Positive science deals with judgments of facts, while ethics is concerned with judgments of Value, specifically determining what is morally right or wrong.

questions.title: A thing is generally said to be good when it is valuable for?
questions.explanation: A thing is generally said to be good when it is valuable for some end or purpose.

questions.title: When we a say that conduct is right, we mean primarily that?
questions.explanation: When we say that conduct is right, we primarily mean that it is according to rule or moral principles.

questions.title: Ethics determines rightness or wrongness of?
questions.explanation: Ethics determines the rightness or wrongness of Human actions.

questions.title: _______deals with logical and semantic questions like "what do we mean by “freedom” and “determinism”.
questions.explanation: Meta ethics deals with logical and semantic questions like "what do we mean by 'freedom' and 'determinism'".

questions.title: The applied dimension of Ethics is known as?
questions.explanation: The applied dimension of Ethics is known as Applied Ethics.

questions.title: Truth, Beauty, and______are considered as the principles of philosophy.
questions.explanation: Truth, Beauty, and Goodness are considered as the principles of philosophy.

questions.title: Ethics is neither a practical science nor an art. This assertion is?
questions.explanation: "Ethics is neither a practical science nor an art" is correct.

questions.title: Positive science concerned with?
questions.explanation: Positive science is concerned with Facts, aiming to describe and explain observable phenomena without making value judgments.

questions.title: Logic deals with the norm of?
questions.explanation: Logic deals with the norm of Truth, focusing on reasoning and valid arguments based on evidence and principles of validity.

questions.title: Ethics deals with the standard of?
questions.explanation: Ethics deals with the standard of Goodness, guiding individuals and societies on what is morally right or wrong.

questions.title: Aesthetics deals with the standard of?
questions.explanation: Aesthetics deals with the standard of Beauty.

questions.title: Normative science deals with?
questions.explanation: Normative science deals with standards or norms, prescribing how things ought to be rather than describing how they are.

questions.title: The term "Good" is connected with the German term?
questions.explanation: The term "Good" is connected with the German term "Gut."

questions.title: Rectus means?
questions.explanation: Rectus primarily means straight.

questions.title: The term "Right" is derived from the Latin?
questions.explanation: The term "Right" is derived from the Latin word "Rectus," which means straight.

questions.title: The word mores means?
questions.explanation: The word "mores" refers to customs or norms that are considered morally significant within a society.

questions.title: Ethos means?
questions.explanation: Ethos primarily means character or the characteristic spirit of a culture, community, or era.

questions.title: Ethics is the science of?
questions.explanation: Ethics is primarily concerned with conduct, guiding individuals and societies on what is considered morally right or wrong.

questions.title: Which was the name of specific Goddess of Quraish?
questions.explanation:
Al-Uzza was the name of specific Goddess of Quraish.

She was associated with fertility, protection, and warfare, and her shrine was located near Mecca.

The worship of Al-Uzza declined after the rise of Islam in the region.

questions.title: In which Juz' (para) of the Holy Quran is Surah Al-Ahzab located?
questions.explanation:
Surah Al-Ahzab is the 33rd chapter of the Quran, located in juz's 21 and 22.

The chapter is named after the parties, or confederates,

who fought the Muslims at the Battle of the Trench in 627.

questions.title: Al-Basir is one of the 99 names of Allah in Islam, and it means
questions.explanation: Al-Basir" (البصير) is one of the 99 names of Allah in Islam, and it means The All-Seeing

questions.title: Who is the current Foreign Secretary of Spain _____?
questions.explanation:
José Manuel Albares has been serving as the 15th and current Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs of Spain since 2021.


last verified & updated on 13-05-2024

questions.title: Which category of following is entitled for Zakat ?
questions.explanation:
32 times the word Zakat occurs in the Holy Quran

22 times word Zakat is used in Makki Surahs

Zakat was made compulsory in 2 A.H.

but its complete system was imposed in the year of 8th hijrah.

questions.title: What is the total number of Kalima in Islam?
questions.explanation:
List of six Kalimas

1 كَلِمَاتْ اَلطَّيِّبَة‎

Kalimat aṭ-Ṭayyibah (Word of Purity)

***

2 كَلِمَاتْ اَلشَّهَادَة‎

Kalimat ash-Shahādah (Word of Testimony)

****

3 كَلِمَاتْ اَلتَّمْجِيدْ‎

Kalimat at-Tamjīd (Word of Majesty)

***

4 كَلِمَاتْ اَلتَّوْحِيدْ‎

Kalimat at-Tawḥīd (Word of Oneness)

****

5 كَلِمَاتْ إِسْتِغْفَارْ‎

Kalimat ʾIstighfār (Word of Penitence)

***

6 كَلِمَاتْ رَدّْ اَلْكُفْرْ‎

Kalimat Radd al-Kufr ("Word of Rejection of Disbelief")

***

questions.title: Hateem is also named as
questions.explanation:
Hijr-Ismail also known as Hateem,

is a low wall originally part of the Kaaba.

questions.title: Mina is also known as ___
questions.explanation:
Mina  also known as Muna 

commonly known as the "City of the Tents"

is a valley located 8 kilometres

Southeast of the city of Mecca, in the district of Masha'er,

Province of Makkah in the Hejazi region Saudi Arabia.

questions.title: During Hajj, pebbles are collected from where ______?
questions.explanation:
At least 49 pebbles are needed for the ritual.

Jamart throwing of pebbles, it is performed on 10th, 11th, 12th and 13th of Zul Hajj.

Pebbles are collected from Muzdalfa a plain southeast of Mina,

questions.title: What is the tittle of Hazrat Ibrahim (AS)?
questions.explanation:
' خليل الله'  which means 'the friend of Allah'

Hazrat Ibrahim (A.S) was born Iraq

Hazrat Ibrahim is buried in Jerusalem, Israel. 

Abraham had two children,

1.      Isaac

2.      Ismael


Hazrat Ibrahim's (AH) name is mentioned in the Holy Quran 67 times.

questions.title: Prophet Muhammad (PBUH) was buried in ___
questions.explanation:
Holy Prophet Hazrat Muhammad (PBUH)'s grand grandfather was Hashim ibn Abd Manaf, for whom the tribe is named. Members of this clan are referred to as Hashmi.

Banu Hashim is a clan in the Quraysh tribe. So the Family of the Prophet Hazrat Muhammad (PBHU) was Hashmi and the Tribe was Quraish.

questions.title: How long was 'Toofan-e-Nooh'?
questions.explanation:
Duration of Tofaan e Noah was around 6 months.

After the 2242 years of Hazrat Adam A.S the event of Toofan-e-Noah occurred.

 About 80 people were with him in the boat.

Hazrat Noah lived for 950 years.

Noah was sent to Iraq.

Nooh is called predecessor, Naji Ullah; Shaikh ul Ambiya.

Abu ul Bashr Sani is title of Noah.

After toofan-e-nooh, the city establish was Khasran

AGM 02.05.2023

questions.title: جنت البقیع سے کیا مراد ہے ؟
questions.explanation:
Jannat al-Baqīi is the oldest and the first Islamic cemetery of Medina


 It is located to the southeast of the Prophet's Mosque,

They contain the graves of some of Prophet Muhammad's family and friends.

It is also known as Baqīʿ al-Gharqad.

questions.title: Which prayer is obligatory (Farz) during the time of Waqoof e Arafat?
questions.explanation:
Pilgrims perform the Zuhr and Asr two prayers together at noon time at Masjid al-Namirah on the Day of Arafah.

This is known as "standing before God" (wuquf) and is one of the most important rites of Hajj.

questions.title: غسل کے آخر میں پورے جسم پر کم از کم ـــــــــــــــــ بار پانی بہایا جائے۔
questions.explanation:
غسل کے تین فرائض ہیں

کلی کرنا

ناک میں پانی ڈالنا

پورے جسم پر پانی ڈالنا


وضو کے کتنے فرض ہیں

چار

questions.title: صبر کے لغوی معنی کیا ہیں
questions.explanation: صبر کا مطلب ہے خاموشی، بردباری، تحمل

questions.title: قرآن کریم کی سورہ احقاف کی آیت نمبر 35 میں اللہ تعالی نے ــــــــــــــــــ کے بارے میں فرمایا ہے۔
questions.explanation: تو تم صبر کرو جیسا ہمت والے رسولوں نے صبر کیا (ف۸۸) اور ان کے لیے جلدی نہ کرو (ف۸۹) گویا وہ جس دن دیکھیں گے (ف۹۰) جو انہیں وعدہ دیا جاتا ہے (ف۹۱) دنیا میں نہ ٹھہرے تھے مگر دن کی ایک گھڑی بھر یہ پہنچانا ہے (ف۹۲) تو کون ہلاک کیے جائیں گے مگر بے حکم لوگ (ف۹۳) ﴿۳۵﴾

questions.title: ہر صاحب استطاعت مسلمان مرد اور عورت پر زندگی میں ایک بار ــــــــــــــــــــــــ فرض ہے۔
questions.explanation: حج نو ہجرہ کو فرض ہوا تھا اور نبی کریم صلی اللہ تعالی علیہ وآلہ وسلم دس ہجری کو پہلی اور آخری مرتبہ حج ادا کیا تھا۔

questions.title: منکرین زکوۃ کے خلاف جہاد کا آغاز کس نے کیا؟
questions.explanation: حضور نبی کریم  کی خبر وفات سے ہر طرف بغاوت کے شعلے بھڑکنے لگے تھے ، بغاوت سب سے زیادہ یمن کےعلاقہ میں تھی ۔ بہت سے جھوٹے نبی نمودار ہو چکے تھے ۔ بنی حنیفہ میں مسیلمہ اور بنی اسد میں طلیحہ نے نبوت کا دعویٰ کر کے ہزاروں لوگوں کو اپنے ساتھ ملا لیا اور ان لوگوں نے زکوۃ دینے سے بھی انکار کردیا۔ان حالات کی وجہ سے زکوۃ نہ دینے والوں کے خلاف جہاد کا علان کیا گیا

questions.title: Against whom the Muslim ruler and Warrior Salahuddin Ayubi fought?
questions.explanation:
Crusaders صلیبی

Salah ud Din Ayubi from Kurd generation.

Sultan Salahuddin Ayubi was the legendary warrior who fought the Crusaders for a long time and

at last, overcame them and gained victory before he pushed back the joined powers of Europe away from the Holy Land.

سلطان صلاح الدین ایوبی وہ عظیم جنگجو تھے جنہوں نے طویل عرصے تک صلیبیوں کا مقابلہ کیا اور

آخر کار، ان پر قابو پا لیا اور فتح حاصل کی اس سے پہلے کہ اس نے یورپ کی مشترکہ طاقتوں کو مقدس سرزمین سے پیچھے دھکیل دیا۔

questions.title: ?ایک اچھا اخلاقی کردار کیا ہے
questions.answer: اچھائی کرنا کیوں کہ یہ درست عمل ہے
questions.explanation:
?ایک اچھا اخلاقی کردار کیا ہے

اچھائی کرنا کیوں کہ یہ درست عمل ہے

questions.title: ایک اچھا انسان کیسے بن سکتا ہے
questions.explanation:
ایک اچھا انسان کیسے بن سکتا ہے

 اللہ کی حکم مان کے

questions.answer: ان کو توڑا نہیں جا سکتا

قدرت کے قوانین اخلاقی قوانین سے کیسے مختلف ہوتے ہیں

ان کو توڑا نہیں جا سکتا

questions.title: عام دنوں میں کی گئی عبادت سے رمضان میں کتنا زیادہ ثواب ملتا ہے؟
questions.explanation: عام دنوں میں کی گئی عبادت سے رمضان میں  ستر گنا زیادہ ثواب ملتا ہے

questions.title: حضرت میکائیل کے ذمے کیا کام ہے
questions.explanation: حضرت میکائیل کے ذمے  بارش برسانا اور رزق پہنچانا

questions.title: Opposite of Tauheed is__________?
questions.explanation:
Tawhid is the indivisible oneness concept of monotheism in Islam.

توحید اسلام میں توحید کا ناقابل تقسیم وحدانیت کا تصور ہے۔

questions.title: Hazrat ___________ was the foster mother of Holy Prophet.
questions.explanation:
Hazrat Halimah and her husband were from the tribe of Sa'd.

3 foster mothers of the Holy Prophet

  1.        Hazrat Halema Saadia (RA)    
  2.      Hazrat Khola  (RA)
  3.        Hazrat Suwaiba (RA)

questions.title: Kalmah is__________ pillar of Islam?
questions.explanation:
There are the Five Pillars of Islam:

1) Shahadatain (Touheed) (Kalmah)

2) Salat

3) Zakat

4) Soam

5) Hajj

questions.title: The major types of ethical issues include except?
questions.explanation:
The three major types of ethical issues

1.       Systematic issues

2.       Corporate issues

3.       Individual issues

questions.title: In Islam, Obligations are called _____________?
questions.explanation:
In Islam, there are five key obligations (Five Pillars of Islam) upon each Muslim.

Five key obligations Consist of Shahadah, Salah, Zakat, Sawm and Hajj.

questions.title: It is important to have a good _______ to be successful in your career.
questions.explanation: It is important to have a good Attitude to be successful in your career.

questions.title: What is the verbal meaning of word “islam”?
questions.explanation: Islam means "total surrender to the will of Allah".
